does: This paper introduces ContextualJailbreak, a black-box red-teaming method that uses evolutionary search to automatically generate multi-turn conversational attacks against large language models. The approach simulates dialogue exchanges where earlier turns prime the model toward unsafe responses, using a two-level judge that assigns harm scores (0-5) to guide the search process. By treating partially harmful outputs as useful signals rather than binary failures, the method iteratively refines conversational scaffolds to discover effective jailbreaks that exploit contextual priming vulnerabilities more efficiently than prior single-turn or manual approaches.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that multi-turn conversational context creates a significant jailbreak surface complementary to single-turn prompt injection attacks. It extends prior evolutionary red-teaming methods—which have dominated single-turn optimization—into the multi-turn setting by addressing the challenge of searching over dialogue sequences rather than isolated prompts. The paper directly responds to limitations in existing search-based approaches by exploring the design space of mutators that effectively manipulate conversational priming, while leveraging insights from work on graded reward signals (beyond binary success/failure) to enable more nuanced guidance of the search process.
enables: This approach enables researchers to automatically discover new vulnerability patterns in how LLMs process conversational context, potentially revealing systematic weaknesses in safety training that rely on turn-level alignment. The graded harm scoring framework and mutator design principles could extend to other dialogue-based vulnerabilities beyond jailbreaking. Future work can scale these methods to search longer conversation chains, investigate why certain priming patterns prove effective, and develop corresponding defenses that account for conversational continuity. The automation also supports systematic red-teaming at scale, helping identify which model architectures or training procedures are most susceptible to contextual manipulation.
